skill.name: gitops-workflow
skill.description: Implement GitOps workflows with ArgoCD and Flux for automated, declarative Kubernetes deployments with continuous reconciliation.
skill.summary: A comprehensive guide for implementing GitOps practices using ArgoCD and Flux CD to automate Kubernetes deployments with declarative configuration and continuous reconciliation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 97 patterns but all are false positives. The skill contains documentation (markdown files) with bash command examples for ArgoCD and Flux installation. External command patterns are legitimate kubernetes CLI operations in reference docs, not executable code. Network URLs point to official vendor distributions. Base64 usage is for Kubernetes secret encoding (standard practice), not cryptography.

low_findings.title: External Command Execution in Documentation
low_findings.description: Bash commands present in markdown documentation for kubernetes CLI operations. These are instructional examples, not executable code, but users should verify commands before running.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed these are markdown code blocks containing kubectl/helm/argocd CLI commands for documentation purposes only.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Vendor URLs
low_findings.description: Official ArgoCD and Flux distribution URLs are hardcoded for installation. These point to legitimate vendor repositories but should be pinned to specific versions for reproducibility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to official argoproj.github.io and fluxcd.io domains - standard practice for k8s tool installation.

content.user_title: Implement GitOps workflows with ArgoCD and Flux
content.value_statement: Managing Kubernetes deployments manually leads to configuration drift and inconsistent environments. This skill enables automated, declarative deployments using Git as the single source of truth with continuous reconciliation.
actual_capabilities: Guide ArgoCD installation and initial cluster configuration
Create Flux CD bootstrap configurations for GitHub repositories
Design repository structures following GitOps best practices
Configure sync policies with automated prune and self-heal options
Implement progressive delivery strategies including canary and blue-green deployments
Set up secret management using External Secrets Operator and Sealed Secrets
limitations: Requires existing Kubernetes cluster access and kubectl configuration
Does not execute commands directly - provides documentation and configuration examples
GitHub or Git repository access required for Flux bootstrap operations
Secret management solutions require external services like AWS Secrets Manager

use_cases.title: Platform Engineer Setting Up GitOps Infrastructure
use_cases.description: Establish a complete GitOps pipeline for a new Kubernetes cluster with ArgoCD, including RBAC, SSO integration, and multi-environment deployment strategies.

use_cases.title: DevOps Team Automating Application Deployments
use_cases.description: Migrate from manual kubectl deployments to automated Git-based workflows with approval gates for production and automated sync for staging environments.

use_cases.title: SRE Implementing Progressive Delivery
use_cases.description: Configure canary deployments with Argo Rollouts to gradually shift traffic and validate new releases before full production rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Basic ArgoCD Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me install ArgoCD on my Kubernetes cluster and configure the initial admin access. I need the basic installation manifests and steps to access the UI.

prompt_templates.title: Repository Structure Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Git repository structure for managing Kubernetes deployments across three environments: development, staging, and production. Include separate directories for applications and infrastructure components.

prompt_templates.title: Sync Policy Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create ArgoCD Application manifests with sync policies that enable automated sync for staging but require manual approval for production. Include retry policies and sync windows for maintenance periods.

prompt_templates.title: Progressive Delivery Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure a canary deployment strategy using Argo Rollouts that shifts 20% traffic initially, pauses for validation, then progressively increases to 100%. Include health checks and automatic rollback conditions.

output_examples.input: Set up ArgoCD for a new Kubernetes cluster
output_examples.output: Complete installation guide with kubectl commands for namespace creation, manifest application, initial password retrieval, and CLI configuration steps.

output_examples.input: Create a GitOps application manifest for production
output_examples.output: ArgoCD Application YAML with source repository reference, target path, destination cluster, sync policy with prune and self-heal enabled, and namespace creation option.

output_examples.input: Implement secret management without storing secrets in Git
output_examples.output: External Secrets Operator configuration with SecretStore reference to AWS Secrets Manager, including refresh interval and secret mapping specification.

best_practices: Describe applications declaratively in Git and avoid manual kubectl changes that create configuration drift
Store secrets outside Git using External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets for sensitive configuration
Require manual approval for production syncs while allowing automated sync for non-production environments
anti_patterns: Commit plaintext secrets or credentials to Git repositories even in private repos
Enable automated sync to production without approval gates or sync windows
Mix infrastructure and application manifests in the same directory without clear separation

faq.question: What is the difference between ArgoCD and Flux?
faq.answer: Both implement GitOps but differ in architecture. ArgoCD provides a UI and uses a pull-based model with Application CRDs. Flux is CLI-focused and uses a modular controller approach. Choose ArgoCD for UI-driven workflows or Flux for CLI-centric operations.

faq.question: How do I handle secrets in GitOps?
faq.answer: Never commit plaintext secrets to Git. Use External Secrets Operator to fetch from AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or HashiCorp Vault. Alternatively, use Sealed Secrets to encrypt secrets before committing.

faq.question: Can I use GitOps with existing manual deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. Import existing resources into Git by exporting current manifests, then configure ArgoCD or Flux to manage them. Enable self-heal to automatically revert manual changes.

faq.question: How do I rollback a failed deployment?
faq.answer: Revert the Git commit that introduced the change. The GitOps controller will detect the change and sync the cluster to the previous state automatically. You can also use argocd app rollback or flux suspend commands.

faq.question: What is the App of Apps pattern?
faq.answer: A pattern where a parent ArgoCD Application manages multiple child Applications. This enables bootstrapping entire environments from a single Application definition and organizing related applications hierarchically.

faq.question: How do I manage multiple clusters with GitOps?
faq.answer: Use ArgoCD to manage multiple destination clusters by specifying different server URLs in Application manifests. For Flux, bootstrap each cluster separately or use Flux multi-tenancy with cluster selectors.
